Issue body actions

Hi,

I’ve been experimenting with [Montreal Forced Aligner (MFA)](https://github.com/MontrealCorpusTools/Montreal-Forced-Aligner) and WhisperX on the same `.wav` audio file to compare word-level timestamp accuracy.

While MFA produces highly accurate word-level timestamps, I’ve noticed that the timestamps generated by WhisperX are significantly off. I’ve attached a comparison table (see image) showing the differences in word boundaries between MFA and WhisperX for the same audio.

**My WhisperX command:**

```
whisperx source.wav --model large-v3 --language en --align_model WAV2VEC2_ASR_LARGE_LV60K_960H --highlight_words True
```

**Questions:**

- Given forced alignment is an intended use case for WhisperX, am I missing any recommended settings or workflow to improve alignment accuracy?

I was expecting WhisperX to generate identical word-level timestamps. I have used Praat to visualize the MFA output and found it to be very accurate.

Any guidance or clarification would be greatly appreciated!

Thank you.

![Image](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/39008835/497319816-485ee175-2967-4067-b6e4-008c97eff58e.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..Lp75w32NGR4fqKLJ8ihwdN23D4_ve-I5fr_tzvwrQrc)

I can confirm that WhisperX alignment seems to be way off. What I seeing (hearing :)) on my end are timestamps starting in the middle of the first word of sentences. In a particular case, the sentence starts after a long period of silence, and timestamp is still way off. We can clearly see how much it is off by loading the audio on Audacity (or whatever.)

I tried different models and also playing with VAD options, like loweing the `vad-onset` value, but it didn't help.

edit: tested Montreal-Forced-Aligner

The good: MFA did a much better job on the problematic sentences.

The bad: unfortunately MFA comes with its own set of problems.

- Installing it without conda requires a priest, 10 Buddhists monks, and sacrificing 20 virgins.
- one of my problematic sentences started with `1. Avocado banana`. MFA gave the `1.` a very precise timestamp, but replaced the text with `<unk>`. At first I thought it didn't support numerals, but another similar sentence started with `10. Carrot death` and this time it had no problem with the `10.`

I've been facing the same problems. Decided to give `gentle` a try and it performed extremely well.

Just like `MFA`, sometimes it does not understand a word and replaces it by `<unk>`. Unlike `MFA`, `gentle` gives you the original word too, so you can just use that and ignore the `<unk>`

The biggest problem with `gentle` is that it also relies on `kaldi`, which is not a package most distros carry. It is not that hard to build it, and `gentle` comes with a `install.sh` script that will do it all for you. But the script needs some light-patching to work and building kaldi, even if you patch the script to build only what is really required, takes a quite a while.

The good news is that a static build (with CUDA enabled, O3, march and mtune native, omit-frame-pointer and no debug info) can be compressed to 15MB. I think LTO might makes things even better, but I forgot to enable it.

I'm thinking maybe we could add a new option to use a different aligner and leave it to the user to pass a path to the required binaries.

WhisperX is a library built on top of OpenAI Whisper to bring Word-level Timestamps for your audio transcription. This uses a technique called "forced alignment"

Whisper-Based Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) with improved timestamp accuracy using forced alignment.

I use OpenAI's [Whisper](https://github.com/openai/whisper) python lib for speech recognition. How can I get word-level timestamps?

* * *

To transcribe with OpenAI's [Whisper](https://github.com/openai/whisper) (tested on Ubuntu 20.04 x64 LTS with an Nvidia GeForce RTX 3090):

```python
Copy
conda create -y --name whisperpy39 python==3.9
conda activate whisperpy39
pip install git+https://github.com/openai/whisper.git
sudo apt update && sudo apt install ffmpeg
whisper recording.wav
whisper recording.wav --model large
```

If using an Nvidia GeForce RTX 3090, add the following after `conda activate whisperpy39`:

```python
Copy
pip install -f https://download.pytorch.org/whl/torch_stable.html
conda install pytorch==1.10.1 torchvision torchaudio cudatoolkit=11.0 -c pytorch
```

I created a repo to recover word-level timestamps (and confidence), and also more accurate segment timestamps:
[https://github.com/Jeronymous/whisper-timestamped](https://github.com/Jeronymous/whisper-timestamped)

It is built based on the cross-attention weights of Whisper, as in [this notebook in the Whisper repo](https://github.com/openai/whisper/blob/f82bc59f5ea234d4b97fb2860842ed38519f7e65/notebooks/Multilingual_ASR.ipynb). I tuned a bit the approach to get better location, and added the possibility to get the cross-attention on the fly, so there is no need to run the Whisper model twice. There is no memory issue when processing long audio.

Note: first, I tried the approach of using wav2vec model to realign Whisper's transcribed words to input audio. It works reasonably well, but it has many drawbacks : it needs to handle a separate (wav2vec) model, to perform another inference on the full signal, to have one wav2vec model per language, to normalize the transcribed text so that the set of characters fits the one of wav2vec model (e.g. converting numbers in characters, symbols like "%", currencies...). Also the alignment can have troubles on disfluencies that are usually removed by Whisper (so part of what would recognize wav2vec model is missing, like start of sentences that are reformulated).

In `openai-whisper` version `20231117`, you can get word level timestamps by setting `word_timestamps=True` when calling `transcribe()`:

```python
Copy
pip install openai-whisper
```

```python
Copy
import whisper
model = whisper.load_model("large")
transcript = model.transcribe(
    word_timestamps=True,
    audio="toto.mp3"
)
for segment in transcript['segments']:
    print(''.join(f"{word['word']}[{word['start']}/{word['end']}]"
                    for word in segment['words']))
```

prints:

> Toto,\[2.98/3.4\] I\[3.4/3.82\] have\[3.82/3.96\] a\[3.96/4.02\] feeling\[4.02/4.22\] we're\[4.22/4.44\] not\[4.44/4.56\] in\[4.56/4.72\] Kansas\[4.72/5.14\] anymore.\[5.14/5.48\]

[https://openai.com/blog/whisper/](https://openai.com/blog/whisper/) only mentions "phrase-level timestamps", I infer from it that word-level timestamps are not obtainable without adding more code.

[From one of the Whisper authors](https://github.com/openai/whisper/discussions/3#discussioncomment-3703465):

> Getting word-level timestamps are not directly supported, but it could be possible using the predicted distribution over the timestamp tokens or the cross-attention weights.

[https://github.com/jianfch/stable-ts](https://github.com/jianfch/stable-ts) (MIT License):

> This script modifies methods of Whisper's model to gain access to the predicted timestamp tokens of each word without needing addition inference. It also stabilizes the timestamps down to the word level to ensure chronology.

Note that:

- Unclear how precise these word-level timestamps are.
- [subtitles sometimes go out of sync](https://github.com/openai/whisper/discussions/89).

* * *

Another option: use some [word-level forced alignment program](https://linguistics.stackexchange.com/q/45207/2680). E.g., [Lhotse](https://github.com/lhotse-speech/lhotse) (Apache-2.0 license) has [integrated](https://github.com/openai/whisper/discussions/209#discussion-4436246) both Whisper ASR and Wav2vec forced alignment:

Improving Whisper Timestamps for PHI Redaction==============================================Problem:\-\-\------Whisper's word-level timestamps are often coarse or inaccurate, making precise PHI redaction difficult.Solutions:==========1\. Use WhisperX (Recommended)\-\-\---------------------------\- WhisperX is an extension of Whisper that adds forced alignment for accurate word-level timestamps.\- It uses Whisper for transcription and aligns words using phoneme models like Wav2Vec2.\- Produces highly accurate timestamps (e.g., "Varun" from 0.96s to 1.20s)\- Supports speaker diarization as well.GitHub: https://github.com/m-bain/whisperxSample output:\[{"word": "My", "start": 0.45, "end": 0.65},{"word": "name", "start": 0.66, "end": 0.83},{"word": "is", "start": 0.84, "end": 0.95},{"word": "Varun", "start": 0.96, "end": 1.20}\]2\. Gentle Forced Aligner\-\-\----------------------\- Takes in a transcript and audio, and returns high-precision timestamps.\- Ideal if transcript is already generated.\- Works well for redaction and alignment.GitHub: https://github.com/lowerquality/gentle3\. Faster-Whisper + Forced Alignment\-\-\----------------------------------\- Use Faster-Whisper for transcription (faster and better alignment than Whisper)\- Pair it with alignment tools like WhisperX, Gentle, or Aeneas.4\. Phoneme-level VAD Postprocessing\-\-\---------------------------------\- Use Voice Activity Detection and phoneme segmentation to fine-tune alignment.\- Helps correct timestamp boundaries around PHI.Recommended Pipeline for PHI Audio Redaction:=============================================1\. Transcribe audio: Whisper or Faster-Whisper2\. Align timestamps: WhisperX (best), Gentle (fallback)3\. Detect PHI: spaCy, Clinical BERT, or GPT4\. Redact audio: pydub or ffmpeg5\. \[Optional\] Re-synthesize audio: Bark, SpeechT5Bonus: WhisperX Sample Setup============================
